steps.title: Spot the internal comma in the appositive phrase
steps.content:
The phrase “such as 'karst,' which refers to limestone terrain riddled with caves or sinkholes” provides examples of the terms and is a non-restrictive appositive.

The key signal is the internal comma after “karst,” which determines what punctuation can properly separate this phrase from the noun.

steps.title: Apply the punctuation rule for appositives with internal punctuation
steps.content:
A dash is required to separate an appositive phrase containing internal commas from its noun, preventing the external punctuation from merging with those internal commas and blurring the phrase boundary.

A comma would create confusion with the internal comma; a colon doesn't function for appositives; no punctuation leaves the structure unclear.

The dash also echoes the dash before “illustrate” at the sentence's end, framing the appositive phrase.

This confirms C is correct.
